# UC-18.2.11 · NAT Rule Utilization — VMware NSX Policy Tier-0 and Tier-1 NAT Sessions, SNAT Port Pools, and Edge Translation Pressure

## Description

Monitors VMware NSX Policy NAT via GET /policy/api/v1/infra/tier-0s/{tier-0-id}/nat/USER and GET /policy/api/v1/infra/tier-1s/{tier-1-id}/nat/USER plus GET /policy/api/v1/search?query=resource_type:PolicyNatRule. Tracks DNAT, SNAT, REFLEXIVE, NO_DNAT, NO_SNAT with match_destination_network, match_source_network, translated_network, translated_ports and enabled, logging, firewall_match. Aligns per-rule NAT-session counters with GET /policy/api/v1/infra/realized-state/realized-entities/{rule_id}/runtime NAT statistics and GET /api/v1/edge-clusters/{cluster-id}/edge-nodes/{node-id}/nat/translation-statistics-summary port_pool_used versus default 65536 SNAT ports per Tier-0 SR Edge node, plus GET /api/v1/aggregation-service/cluster-statistics. Ingests local6 EDGE-NAT-INFO, EDGE-NAT-DROP, EDGE-NAT-SESSION-LIMIT with Splunkbase 4856 vmware:nsxt:nat, vmware:nsxt:edge_status, vmware:nsxt:syslog and optional gRPC NSX Telemetry NAT counters. Shows port-allocation pressure on NAT-session-table headroom — distinct from DFW, GENEVE, Intelligence flows, BGP peers, or Manager quorum siblings.

## Value

Busy DNAT front doors and SNAT masquerade pools share finite ephemeral ports on each Edge node; Splunk trending ties Policy NAT rule IDs to session_count_active and port_pool_used_percent so NetOps resize Tier-0 SR pools, split noisy rules, or add Edge capacity before SESSION-LIMIT syslog storms strand payroll APIs or checkout carts.

## Implementation

Deploy Splunk_TA_vmware-nsxt ([Splunkbase 4856](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4856)) capturing vmware:nsxt:nat, vmware:nsxt:edge_status, vmware:nsxt:syslog with REST collectors for Policy NAT lists and MP translation-statistics-summary JSON; normalize FIELDALIAS chains for nat_rule_id and tier_id; schedule macros/nsxt_nat_util wrapping the SPL with fifteen-minute bins and throttle pool_alert per nat_rule_id edge_node_id.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install Splunk_TA_vmware-nsxt ([Splunkbase 4856](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4856)) on Search Heads, Heavy Forwarders hosting HTTPS collectors, and syslog receivers terminating Edge local6 feeds for VMware NSX 4.x estates where Policy NAT is enforced on Tier-0 SR instances and Tier-1 distributed routers.
• Provision least-privilege identities whose roles combine infra read scopes on GET /policy/api/v1/infra/tier-0s/{tier-0-id}/nat/USER and GET /policy/api/v1/infra/tier-1s/{tier-1-id}/nat/USER, GET /policy/api/v1/search?query=resource_type:PolicyNatRule enumeration, GET /policy/api/v1/infra/realized-state/realized-entities/{rule_id}/runtime statistics for NAT counters, GET /api/v1/edge-clusters/{cluster-id}/edge-nodes/{node-id}/nat/translation-statistics-summary port telemetry, and GET /api/v1/aggregation-service/cluster-statistics observability — authenticate via POST /api/session/create cookies or mutual TLS, never interactive admin consoles.
• Guarantee TCP 443 paths from collectors to NSX Manager and Policy VIPs without rewriting JSON bodies used by FIELDALIAS overlays; reserve Splunk Connect for Syslog clusters when EDGE-NAT MNEMONIC bursts exceed sustained four-digit EPS on busy edges because NAT syslog alone can dominate indexers when operators toggle logging during exercises.

Quick win: Land vmware:nsxt:nat beside vmware:nsxt:edge_status into one index=vmware alias for fourteen days — chart session_count_active versus port_pool_used_percent before wiring firewall_match or logging escalations — proves Splunk mirrors the Translation Statistics Summary inside one sprint.

Step 1 — Data sources
Collect authoritative Policy intent plus dataplane realization so Splunk trends reflect DNAT, SNAT, REFLEXIVE, NO_DNAT, and NO_SNAT semantics separately instead of collapsing everything into anonymous syslog counts. Tier-0 NAT rules terminate on the provider Tier-0 service router scope in many designs, while Tier-1 NAT rules ride the tenant Tier-1 router object; the JSON shape is similar, yet capacity and port-pool placement differ—keep them separate in every panel and lookup.

• GET /policy/api/v1/infra/tier-0s/{tier-0-id}/nat/USER — Returns central Tier-0 NAT rulesets under the USER bucket (contrast AUTO defaults where present) including per-rule paths for match_destination_network, match_source_network, translated_network, translated_ports, plus enabled, logging, and firewall_match attributes that steer whether Edge dataplane emits drop copies alongside session telemetry. Index JSON lines as vmware:nsxt:nat (or companion sourcetype your TA tags) keyed by nat_rule_id and tier_id pointing at the Tier-0 logical object.
• GET /policy/api/v1/infra/tier-1s/{tier-1-id}/nat/USER — Same schema for downstream tenant Tier-1 routers; keep separate dashboards because SNAT pools and DNAT front doors may share Edge clusters with unrelated load-balancer VIPs—never mix tenant tokens when Splunk macros fan out alerts.
• GET /policy/api/v1/search?query=resource_type:PolicyNatRule — Fleet-wide listing to backfill nat_rule_id when only UUID fragments appear inside syslog messages; nightly mirrors keep CMDB attachments honest after Policy refactors rename display_name but preserve path UUIDs.
• GET /policy/api/v1/infra/realized-state/realized-entities/{rule_id}/runtime — Supplies NAT-stats counters (per-rule session totals, realization errors) feeding high-water alerts when Policy intent diverges from Edge runtime; this is where realized-state alerting catches partial publish or Edge version skew before user tickets mention “one-way audio” or stalled API calls.
• GET /api/v1/edge-clusters/{cluster-id}/edge-nodes/{node-id}/nat/translation-statistics-summary — Manager-plane view of port_pool_used, port_pool_total (default 65536 SNAT ephemeral ports per Edge node on Tier-0 SR designs per VMware capacity guidance—adjust when Edge VM form factor or bare-metal SKU changes published pool math), port_pool_used_percent, and per-service-router NAT allocation. Without this REST family, Splunk guesses from syslog alone—unsafe for exhaustion math.
• GET /api/v1/aggregation-service/cluster-statistics — Confirms stats-aggregator health so Manager dashboards and Splunk agree; empty JSON here often precedes phantom zero-session anomalies in the UI while dataplane counters still move.
• Syslog — Facility local6 lines carrying EDGE-NAT-INFO, EDGE-NAT-DROP, and EDGE-NAT-SESSION-LIMIT MNEMONIC tokens alongside Splunkbase 4856 sourcetypes vmware:nsxt:syslog, enriched vmware:nsxt:nat, and vmware:nsxt:edge_status heartbeat exports. Tie local6 routing to Heavy Forwarders that preserve PRI headers so TA transforms remain stable.
• gRPC NSX Telemetry — Optional NSX Telemetry sensor stream for per-Edge NAT counter families when HTTPS polling alone misses sub-minute spikes on Tier-0 SR hot paths during retail or payroll cutover surges.

Step 2 — Field extractions
Normalize FIELDALIAS chains so nat_rule_id, tier_id (Tier-0 or Tier-1 policy path), nat_action (DNAT, SNAT, REFLEXIVE), match_destination, match_source, translated_network, translated_ports, enabled, logging, firewall_match, session_count_active, port_pool_used, port_pool_total, port_pool_used_percent, nat_drop_packets, edge_node_id, and edge_cluster_id reconcile Policy camelCase payloads, REST summaries, and syslog fragments. Duplicate resources break per-rule trending—especially after Tier-1 segments move between projects but Edge caches still show old bindings for minutes.

Step 3 — Initial validation
Cross-check Splunk aggregates against get nat translations Edge CLI tables for the same edge_node_id, NSX UI **NAT** Translation Statistics Summary panes, **Aggregation Service** spot metrics, and vRealize Network Insight **NAT**-rule cards. Disagreements usually mean stale **Policy** snapshots or API pagination gaps, not harmless noise. When CLI shows active bindings but Splunk remains zero, inspect **HEC** batching delays before rewriting parsers.

Step 4 — SPL building blocks
• Per-rule session-count trending — timechart avg(**session_count_active**) by **nat_rule_id** with nat_action facet to expose **DNAT** front-door storms versus **SNAT** masquerade load.
• Port-pool headroom — Track **port_pool_used_percent** with sustained greater-than-eighty-five-percent windows on any **edge_node_id** feeding **Tier-0** **SR** **SNAT** pools; default 65536 ports per **Edge** node sets worst-case mathematics for collision-prone workloads that burst ephemeral sockets.
• **NAT**-drop-rate detection — Summarize nat_drop_packets from **vmware:nsxt:syslog** EDGE-**NAT**-DROP lines joined to **session_count_active** so pure **syslog** storms do not page without session stress on the dataplane.
• Top-talker ranking — eventstats perc95 per **nat_rule_id**, flag rows where live averages exceed one-hundred-twenty percent of baseline to spotlight rule-specific appetite versus fleet-wide averages.
• Per-**Tier-0** **SR** aggregate headroom — stats max(**port_pool_used_percent**) by **edge_cluster_id** and **edge_node_id** to show **SR**-class **NAT** session-table pressure before individual rules max out—critical when dozens of **DNAT** entries fan into one **SNAT** exit.
• Port-pool exhaustion forecasting — Derive slope on **port_pool_used_percent** with predict or export metrics to **Splunk ITSI** KPIs for linear runout ETA when onboarding adds **DNAT** fan-out that competes with **SNAT** ephemeral consumption. Port-allocation algorithm choice—deterministic hashing versus random pools—changes how **translated_ports** binds collide with concurrent session churn and can shrink effective headroom even when arithmetic pool size looks fine on paper.

Step 5 — Comprehensive enrichment patterns (deepest section)
• vCenter joins — Map **Splunk_TA_vmware** ([**Splunkbase** 1810](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1810)) vmware:vmware:vsphere:vcenter inventories to translated_network endpoints so saturation ties to application squads instead of anonymous prefixes lingering inside spreadsheets.
• **CMDB** tenant owners — Drive lookups from **Tier-1** attachment metadata (logical_switch tags, segment names) so **nat_rule_id** rows inherit accountable owners when **SNAT** pools thrash during quarter-end batch windows Finance refuses to reschedule.
• **ServiceNow** **CHG** overlays — Join **CHG** payloads referencing **NAT** pushes or **Tier-1** publishes so benign spikes during app-onboarding windows suppress **Sev-2** noise while preserving audit trails for auditors demanding proof Splunk respected maintenance calendars.
• Logging granularity governance — **logging** toggles per rule directly explode **vmware:nsxt:syslog** volume; align Splunk throttles with **firewall_match** policies so observability modes do not starve disk budgets during penetration-test rehearsals.
• ITSI forecasting — Feed **port_pool_used_percent** KPIs into **Splunk ITSI** adaptive thresholds for port-exhaustion ETA narratives tied to **edge_node_id** — distinct from generic CPU KPIs documented in UC-18.2.4 because **NAT** tables exhaust independently from **DPDK** throughput panels.
• MITRE T1090.001 — Flag **SNAT** rules that suddenly proxy unusual internal pairs when match_source_network widens toward broad **RFC1918** supernets by correlating **session_count_active** jumps with Identity or proxy attribution elsewhere — marks potential internal relay abuse distinct from legitimate **DNAT** front doors during blue-green cutovers.
• **Splunk ES** risk objects — Promote saturating **nat_rule_id** rows into risk.notable entries enriched with tier_id context so SOC sees **NAT** misuse attempts alongside firewall narratives without duplicating **Distributed Firewall** UC-18.2.1 verdict logic.
• **Edge** dataplane CPU cross-check — Join modest **vmware:nsxt:edge_status** dataplane_cpu samples to detect **NAT**-rule eviction or connection-retry storms that amplify CPU independently of port_pool math — avoids misattributing drops to pools when the **DPDK** graph is simply hot after a surge of short-lived flows.
• Native load balancer correlation — When **Tier-1** **NAT** shares **Edge** nodes with lb-services VIPs described in VMware load-balancer administration guidance, overlay LB member flaps to explain **SNAT** churn from health-probe storms — separate mechanics from pure **NAT** exhaustion that still deserves its own bridge.

Governance reminders: Document which teams may request **NO_DNAT** or **NO_SNAT** overrides because those bypass expectations and can hide misrouted packets until **realized-state** counters scream. Keep immutable copies of **Policy** JSON snapshots adjacent to Splunk CSV exports whenever **Sev-1** bridges close so postmortems compare intent versus reality without relying on fading operator memory.

Step 6 — Operational runbook
• **NAT** **port-pool** exhaustion — **Sev-2** page when **port_pool_used_percent** stays at or above eighty-five percent for fifteen minutes on any **edge_node_id** tied to production **Tier-0** **SR** pools; include **MTTR** target under forty-five minutes to add **Edge** capacity, redistribute **SNAT** rules across **locale services**, or shrink overly wide **translated_ports** windows per app SOP.
• App-onboarding review — Before **CHG** closes, verify new **DNAT** or **SNAT** entries include realistic **session_count_active** ceilings, **logging** posture, and **firewall_match** expectations — **MTBF** goal exceeds ninety days between repeat false SESSION-LIMIT pages caused by spreadsheet math errors alone.
• Dashboard tokens — Publish **Tier-0** and **Tier-1** variants sharing tokens tier_scope, **edge_cluster_id**, **nat_rule_id** so operators pivot Translation Statistics Summary screenshots into Splunk without rebuilding panels nightly—panels cover per-rule session-count, **port-pool**-headroom heatmap, EDGE-**NAT**-DROP trend, top-talker **NAT** rules, and per-**Edge** aggregate tiles mandated by observability reviews.
• Evidence pack — Archive CSV plus PNG bundles whenever **realized-state** runtime JSON disagrees with **Edge** CLI get nat translations output — proves whether **Policy** drift or dataplane fatigue drives the incident without mixing in Intelligence flow analytics.

Escalation etiquette: Route **NAT** exhaustion bridges to **Edge** platform owners first, then tenant app teams if **translated_ports** must narrow; route **NAT** drop surges tied to **EDGE-**NAT**-DROP** without pool pressure to dataplane engineering for **DPDK** inspection. Document every rehearsal where **logging** spikes EPS so finance can budget indexers quarterly.


## SPL

```spl
(index=vmware OR index=vmware_nsx OR index=network_ops)
(
    sourcetype="vmware:nsxt:nat"
    OR sourcetype="vmware:nsxt:edge_status"
    OR (sourcetype="vmware:nsxt:syslog" AND (
        match(lower(_raw),"(?i)edge-nat-(info|drop|session-limit)")
        OR match(_raw,"EDGE-NAT-INFO")
        OR match(_raw,"EDGE-NAT-DROP")
        OR match(_raw,"EDGE-NAT-SESSION-LIMIT")
    ))
)
earliest=-4h@m
| eval nat_rule_id=trim(coalesce(nat_rule_id, policy_nat_rule_id, rule_id, resource_id, nsx_nat_rule_uuid, ""))
| eval tier_id=trim(coalesce(tier_id, tier0_path, tier1_path, tier_0_uuid, tier_1_uuid, logical_router_path, ""))
| eval nat_action=upper(trim(coalesce(nat_action, nat_type, policy_nat_action, "")))
| eval session_count_active=tonumber(coalesce(session_count_active, active_nat_sessions, nat_session_count, current_sessions, "0"))
| eval port_pool_used_percent=tonumber(coalesce(port_pool_used_percent, nat_port_pool_pct, snat_pool_pct, used_nat_pool_pct, "0"))
| eval edge_node_id=trim(coalesce(edge_node_id, edge_node_uuid, edge_transport_node_id, entity_id, hostname, ""))
| eval nat_drop_packets=tonumber(coalesce(nat_drop_packets, nat_drops, edge_nat_drops, drop_count, "0"))
| eval pool_headroom=round(100-port_pool_used_percent,3)
| eval logging_flag=upper(trim(coalesce(logging, nat_logging, log_enabled, "")))
| eval fw_match=upper(trim(coalesce(firewall_match, nat_fw_match, "MATCH")))
| eval nat_stress=case(
    port_pool_used_percent>=85 AND session_count_active>0, "nat_pool_hot",
    nat_drop_packets>0 AND session_count_active>1000, "nat_drop_pressure",
    session_count_active>60000, "nat_session_spike",
    1=1, null())
| rex field=_raw "(?i)rule(?::|id|=)\\s*(?<rex_nat_rule>[0-9a-fA-F\\-]{8,})"
| eval nat_rule_id=if(len(nat_rule_id)==0 AND len(rex_nat_rule)>0, rex_nat_rule, nat_rule_id)
| where len(nat_rule_id)>0 OR isnotnull(nat_stress)
| bin _time span=15m
| stats avg(session_count_active) as avg_sess max(session_count_active) as peak_sess max(port_pool_used_percent) as peak_pool_pct min(pool_headroom) as min_pool_headroom sum(nat_drop_packets) as sum_nat_drops latest(tier_id) as tier_scope latest(nat_action) as nat_action_latest latest(logging_flag) as logging_flag latest(fw_match) as fw_match dc(edge_node_id) as edge_fanout count as evt_volume by _time nat_rule_id edge_node_id
| eventstats perc95(avg_sess) as p95_sess by nat_rule_id
| eval top_talker=if(avg_sess>=1.25*p95_sess AND isnotnull(p95_sess),1,0)
| eval pool_alert=if(peak_pool_pct>=85,1,0)
| eval drop_ratio_watch=if(sum_nat_drops>0 AND avg_sess>2000,1,0)
| eval tier_token=if(match(tier_scope,"(?i)tier.?0"),"T0_NAT","T1_NAT")
| streamstats window=4 global=false avg(peak_pool_pct) as pool_trend_avg by nat_rule_id edge_node_id
| sort -peak_pool_pct -avg_sess
| head 400
| table _time nat_rule_id tier_scope tier_token nat_action_latest edge_node_id avg_sess peak_sess peak_pool_pct pool_trend_avg min_pool_headroom sum_nat_drops top_talker pool_alert drop_ratio_watch logging_flag fw_match edge_fanout evt_volume
```

## Visualization

(1) Timechart — avg_sess and peak_sess by nat_rule_id split nat_action_latest (DNAT vs SNAT vs REFLEXIVE). (2) Heatmap — peak_pool_pct versus edge_node_id with drill to tier_token. (3) Column chart — sum_nat_drops trend filtered when drop_ratio_watch=1. (4) Table — top_talker=1 rows ranked by avg_sess with tier_scope and logging_flag. (5) Single-value — count pool_alert=1 plus sparkline for cluster-wide pool_trend_avg aggregated per edge_node_id. (6) Histogram — edge_fanout distribution showing how many NAT rules touch the same Edge during SNAT exhaustion postmortems.
